title: The Land Behind Perekop - A Crimean Novel

chapters.title: Crimea is diverse but united
chapters.text:
How many nations formed the Crimean identity? Why is Crimean Tatar culture so crucial for the peninsula? Where did the stereotype about Crimea's pro-Russianness come from? Anastasia Levkova interviewed residents and researchers of Crimea from 2012 to 2023. This summary contains many subjective views on political and personal issues, some of which sound controversial, even scandalous. However, this inner freedom — from religion and culture to political expressions — was a characteristic feature of the Crimean people until the Russian invasion.

Crimean Tatars, descendants of Turkic-speaking tribes, founded the Crimean Khanate in 1441 and practiced Islam. The origin of the Karaites dates to the Khazar era, and their religion is based on the Tanakh. The Krymchaks also come from the Khazar Kaganate, but they turned to Judaism. These three peoples are indigenous to Crimea. Apart from them, Ukrainians, Russians, Greeks, Germans, Jews, and Armenians lived there.

The Land Behind Perekop introduces Crimea to those unaware before 2014 and helps them grasp the relationship between the mainland and the peninsula.

You will see the diversity of culture, traditions, and stories that happened to the novel's characters and are familiar to Ukrainians. Discover authentic discussions from the kitchens and streets of Crimea and apartments throughout Ukraine before the start of the Russian-Ukrainian war.

Perhaps this text will help you understand why awareness of your country's political landscape and historical unease are essential in a civil society. You will learn how easy it is to manipulate indifference and ignorance and how to resist it. In the Crimea example, you can see how the Russian propaganda machine works and creates a picture, passing off as wishful thinking. We must learn this history so it won’t repeat.

chapters.title: The complex identity of Crimean inhabitants
chapters.text:
The main character of Anastasia Levkova's novel is Russian. Or rather, that's what she believed until the complicated truth revealed itself. Her father came from Russia to Crimea for military service, where he met her mother. The person she deemed her great-grandfather, with whom she grew up, served in the NKVD. Her real great-grandfather was repressed. Her great-grandmother was in the Akmola camp for wives of traitors to the motherland. But Oksana learns her family’s story in adulthood.

As a child, she read Russian fairy tales, Soviet literature, and newspapers such as Glory of Labor or Lenin and Children. The writings were about integrity and collaboration, partisan movement, and treason.

Such a literary background is generally familiar to those who grew up in Ukraine before the 2000s. Avoiding Russian-language books was almost impossible due to the sheer amount of such publications. Besides, teachers added charm by talking about the greatness of Russian culture and taking children to all-Union poetry contests, as in the case of the novel's heroine.

There were few specialists in the Ukrainian language and literature in the 90s.

Teachers often taught Ukrainian in an uninteresting way, with stereotypical images of suffering serfs, girls in wreaths of sunflowers and viburnum, and men with wide trousers. When a new Ukrainian language teacher arrived at Oksana's school, she had to dispel this prejudiced attitude and myths surrounding the subject.

At the same time, Oksana had been friends with a neighboring family of Crimean Tatars from early childhood. She learned their language and listened to folk tales and lullabies at their house. An essential rule was to avoid Russian in communication: “Ruscha laf etme!” (Don't speak Russian!), the great-grandmother repeated.

Oksana's friend group included Aliye, Khalil, Akhtem, and Lenur, who were Crimean Tatars, Ukrainian Alyona, and Karaite Borys. All of them were friends and exchanged their family traditions. At school, Lenur faced bullying because of his ethnicity. We’ll discuss this topic in more detail shortly.

As a teenager, Oksana joined the debate club. Her teacher, Akhtem, talked about dissidents and the struggle for rights and taught the students how to form a personality, understand the complexities of social and political topics, and unite. He spoke Ukrainian, Russian, and Crimean Tatar languages.

The cultural and social mutual influence consisted of many national traditions in Crimea and the rest of Ukraine. However, the stereotype of the peninsula being “pro-Russian” in politics persisted. Oksana will hear this thesis later in Kyiv. At first, she lacked arguments for these discussions, but speaking to the Crimeans revealed different stories. She reflected on the topic of her origin and formed her opinion. In the end, the conversations and reflections came down to a common denominator — Crimea is in Ukraine, so its residents should study the Ukrainian language, history, and traditions as Ukrainian citizens.

chapters.title: Learning about indigenous cultures is everyone’s duty
chapters.text:
Unlike the multinational friend group in the novel, few were lucky to have such a diverse experience in the 90s. Therefore, stereotypes imposed by the Soviet Union affected the ideas of different regions and peoples and spread in the media, films, and books.

Oksana's mother said the house of her Crimean Tatar friends always smelled of chibereks. However, they only made them on holidays and weekends, when they could purchase the freshest meat.

Chiberek is a Crimean Tatar dish — a meat-filled pastry fried in oil. Yantik is similar but fried without oil.

One of the tasks of the debate club was to write scenarios for hypothetical situations, like a dialogue between the Crimean Tatars and local authorities about their lands, a wedding of a Crimean Tatar and a Ukrainian, or a conversation between Orthodox and Muslims about installing a cross in a village with a mosque. These and other situations are nuances of the context where different ethnic groups coexisted in Crimea.

What did you expect? This is life. This is what is happening in Crimea today. ~ Anastasiia Levkova

Anastasiia Levkova

Since childhood, Oksana had compared her family customs with those of her friends. Aliye's family was always expressive; they gestured a lot and weren’t afraid to cry or raise their voice in a discussion. Instead, Oksana's father convinced her she should restrain herself because “when a person cries, they feel sorry for themselves.” Only over time did she learn to be open and understand her emotions.

After the start of the Russian-Ukrainian war in 2014, the fight for the rights of indigenous peoples intensified. Ukrainians finally understood they could resist Russian propaganda, which tries to equate all ethnicities into one “all-Russian” nation. Unfortunately, the realization came late.

“There is no land beyond Perekop” is a Crimea proverb marking the separation of mainland Ukraine and the peninsula. At one point in the novel, someone jokingly asks which side of the Perekop conversation is about to deny this statement. In fact, there was and is a connection between the lands. Ukrainian men and women and the world community are now studying the context of indigenous peoples in more detail to return the Crimean lands to Ukraine.

Did you know? The Russians spread the myth that the Crimean Tatars betrayed the Soviet Union in World War II by siding with the Nazis. However, many Russians sided with the Nazis while the indigenous peoples fought in the Red Army. Among them is the two-time hero of the Soviet Union, Amethan Sultan, who shot down almost 50 German planes.

chapters.title: The deportation of Crimean Tatars
chapters.text:
Surgyunlik is the most terrible page in the history of the Crimean Tatars and Ukraine. May 18, 1944, marks the beginning of the deportation of the Crimean Tatars, later recognized as genocide.

From 191,044 to 423,100 Crimean Tatars were deported from Crimea over three days by order of Joseph Stalin to the USSR’s remote regions — the northern Trans-Urals and Central Asian republics.

Early in the morning, 32,000 NKVD agents, organized by Lavrentiy Beria, began knocking on doors. According to eyewitnesses, people had 15 minutes to collect their belongings. They were transported in overcrowded wagons for livestock and traveled for two to three weeks. They didn’t have enough water, food, and air. During the stops, the dead were taken out with little to no time to bury them.

According to the official statement from the Soviet government, the Crimean Tatars were deported for desertion at the beginning of the Second World War and collaboration with the Nazis. The most outstanding absurdity of this motivation is that 8,995 military personnel of Crimean Tatar origin who fought in the Red Army were also deported before or immediately after the end of the war.

Upon arrival in distant lands, the Crimean Tatars faced brutality from the local Uzbeks, Russians, and Kazakhs, who called them traitors. People lived in poverty and hunger in large families in settlements and dugouts. Deportees were forced to work in mines and construction.

Between 20% and 46% of the entire Crimean Tatar people died during and in the first years of deportation.

Subsequently, many Armenians, Bulgarians, Greeks, some Karaites, Italians, Romanians, Kurds, Czechs, Hungarians, and Croats were deported from the peninsula. Afterward, many Russians inhabited the land (their share increased to 72%), as did Ukrainians (22%) and Belarusians. All property of the Crimean Tatars, including houses, passed to new owners.

The surviving Crimean Tatars dreamed of returning to their homeland. After Joseph Stalin’s death, they gathered petitions, insisting on this right.

Mustafa Dzhemilev became one of the leaders of the Crimean Tatar human rights movement. Born in 1943, he grew up in exile in Uzbekistan and served multiple sentences for resistance actions. After Ukraine gained independence, he was the head of the Mejlis of the Crimean Tatar people.

Some Crimean Tatars managed to return to Crimea in the late 1960s. They had no housing or land and couldn’t obtain official positions or receive documents.

The government lifted the ban on the return of deported nationalities to their native lands in 1989. The Crimean Tatars became those who constantly built their houses since they never received their former property back.

chapters.title: Political is personal
chapters.text:
Anastasia Levkova's characters repeatedly discuss politics. In childhood, these conversations are naive, overheard by adults, and simplified into emotional experiences. Later, their beliefs combine with arguments and experience. This artistic technique demonstrates the region’s general political landscape.

The tradition of “kitchen politics conversations” formed during the Soviet Union. People believed their homes were one of the only safe places to express their views — in the circle of their closest friends and with minimal risk of eavesdropping. Younger generations inherited the habit.

Oksana and her friends listened to the teachers, gathered data, and spoke with the people around them to share their observations with their loved ones later. When Oksana finished her studies, she went to Kyiv every month and had the same discussions with her boyfriend and his friends.

The prejudices mainland citizens had about Crimea hurt the main character personally. They said Crimeans didn't know Ukrainian and wanted to move to Russia. Oksana went on the path of self-awareness to eventually switch to Ukrainian in everyday life (with Crimean Tatar interspersed).

Interestingly, prejudices against the Crimean Tatars mostly came from the Crimean people, while the stereotypes were less common in mainland Ukraine. Crimean Tatars could not get a job or a residence permit and heard outrageous comparisons with terrorists in their native land purely because of their identity. Russian propaganda permeated everyday life, just as NKVD agents broke into people's homes 50 years before.

Students of different generations drove the Revolution on Granite in 1990 and the Revolution of Dignity (at the beginning of Euromaidan) in 2013. Having learned to formulate their political beliefs, they stood up to defend them.

Political neutrality was foreign to Ukraine, which gained independence through one revolution and fortified it with two more.

Although some citizens remained indifferent, their number gradually decreased with the development of civil society. Each uprising affected more people, with the largest crowd gathering for Maidan.

Oksana went to rallies in Simferopol and regularly came to Kyiv's Maidan. Later, when the dead of the Heavenly Hundred were buried, and the criminal president Viktor Yanukovych fled, Oksana and her friends went to demonstrations in Crimea to express defiance because of rumors about the peninsula’s transfer to Russia. Meanwhile, many Russian politicians arrived to announce the “referendum.”

On February 26, 2014, up to 12,000 people attended a pro-Ukrainian rally near Crimea’s Verkhovna Rada. A minor pro-Russian action gathered nearby, with disguised Russian military personnel. The pressure from pro-Ukrainian protesters made the parliament cancel their meeting regarding Crimea's exit from Ukraine.

On the morning of February 27, Russian special forces, later called “little green men,” seized government buildings in Crimea wearing uniforms without identification marks. Russia had been moving troops to the peninsula for several days, if not weeks.

chapters.title: How Russia came to “save” Crimea
chapters.text:
At the beginning of the Russian invasion, Crimeans deemed it a temporary misunderstanding. Foreign journalists came to Crimea. In the novel, characters who knew English became fixers. All the friends now lived in the same apartment and discussed politics and news with their closest people after work.

In those days, journalists and fixers communicated with people on the streets of Crimean cities. They heard about the terrible “Banderites” — fascists who are allegedly going to Crimea from Kyiv and Lviv. When asked about sources of information, they cited Russian TV channels. At the same time, when the press asked if they wanted to go to Russia, they said Crimea should remain in Ukraine but “without fascism.”

If I could turn to Vladimir Vladimirovich [Putin], I would tell him: protect us from your own military. ~ Anastasiia Levkova

Anastasiia Levkova

While the “little green men” captured Ukrainian military units in Crimea, the “self-defense” (fighters hired by the government) kidnapped and beat Crimean Tatars, activists, and journalists. It was later revealed they tortured their prisoners.

Russian soldiers now openly attacked pro-Ukrainian rallies. In March, the news came out about the torture and death of Reshat Ametov, a Crimean Tatar activist named the first victim of the Russian-Ukrainian war. Foreign journalists left Crimea for security reasons. According to the results of a fictitious “referendum,” Russia “acquired the Republic of Crimea as part of their land.”

Russian speakers switch to the Ukrainian language so that Russia cannot justify invasion by “protecting” Russian-speaking Ukrainians.

All conversations with pro-Russian Crimeans revolved around the content they consumed. Russian TV series, TV channels, and news promoted propaganda and painted a fantastical utopia for years.

Russia tried to create an inter-ethnic conflict in Crimea using provocations. In this case, it would be possible to introduce troops to stop the bloodshed. However, the community strengthened, and the conflict did not occur.

When many peninsula inhabitants had to leave and Russians arrived expecting the promised riches of Crimea, the blue and yellow flag and Ukrainian language became rare and went underground. Many received threats, and talk about the eviction of indigenous peoples appeared again.

Anastasia Levkova's characters separated: one was threatened, one was recruited, some joined the Ukrainian army, some moved, and others stayed.

Many Crimean Tatars remained to maintain a connection with their native land, for which they fought for over a century. Some Crimean Tatars moved to the mainland, educating Ukrainians about their culture and traditions. During the war, those living in Crimea helped Ukraine regain their lands.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Leaving the peninsula, the novel's heroine, Oksana, reflects on her Russian identity with a strong background in Russian literature. Subsequently, she Ukrainianized this identity thoroughly. Hence, the international community raised a question after Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine began: what about the “great Russian culture”?

We must understand that this culture became great for a reason. For centuries, the Russian government supported it with finances and propaganda, establishing the myth of its superiority. Russian literature glorifies and makes you sympathize with the little man who will never stand up to his government and will always be afraid to think and fight against his weakness. Ultimately, Oksana concluded that this sentiment is the crime of Russian culture, not its greatness.

The heroine ponders whether she is forcing herself into the Ukrainian community. After all, she has different roots, growing up in a pro-Russian context, and cannot be proud of childhood memories of holidays in Ukrainian traditions. But, contrary to Russian propaganda, Oksana studied the Ukrainian and Crimean Tatar contexts, which were engraved so profoundly in her mind they began to replace the Russian one. Ultimately, she understands she is connecting with Ukraine in a meaningful way.

Of course, this text cannot convey all the nuances of the Crimean issue. It also did not cover Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine. The names of the heroes and heroines who waged an underground struggle against the occupation authorities will not be mentioned here either. This chapter of the history of Crimea is ongoing, but it will become important in the memory of generations.

Try this



• Listen to the album of Crimean Tatar singer and Eurovision winner Jamala — QIRIM. You can read about the song stories of Crimea on the website of the same name.

• Read the book by journalist Natalie Humenyuk — The Lost Island. A book of reports from the occupied Crimea.

• Watch the Crimean Tatar director Nariman Aliyev's film Home about the return of a father and son to Crimea, where they bury their son and brother who died in the Russian-Ukrainian war.

• If you have the opportunity, visit the establishments of Crimean Tatar cuisine, for example, “Musafir” and “Sofra” in Kyiv and “Crimean Yard” in Lviv.

Read it till the end?
